title: Alcaraz vs. Sinner: Who is the More Complete Player?
text:
For about two decades, men’s tennis was defined by three names: Rafael Nadal, Roger Federer and Novak Djokovic. But a new generation is here with Carlos Alcaraz and Jannik Sinner dominating the ATP ranks as the torch gets passed.
These two players are quite comparable:
They both turned pro in 2018 at the ages of 14 (Alcaraz) and 16 (Sinner). Their current ATP tour ranks are No. 1 (Alcaraz) and No. 2 (Sinner). Even their head-to-head record is tight, with Alcaraz winning 10 of 16 total matchups.
Whenever Alcaraz and Sinner face off, it almost guarantees a high-level, competitive match, often stretching to all five sets and lasting for hours, showcasing both players’ endurance and mental resilience.
But beyond the excitement of their head-to-head rivalry, who is the more complete player overall?
To start, we’ll take a look at their performance against other top ranking players.

  read_csv("atp_matches_2020.csv"),
  read_csv("atp_matches_2021.csv"),
  read_csv("atp_matches_2022.csv"),
  read_csv("atp_matches_2023.csv"),
  read_csv("atp_matches_2024.csv"))

Code
players <- c("Carlos Alcaraz", "Jannik Sinner")

rank_tiers <- all_matches %>%
  filter(winner_name %in% players | loser_name %in% players) %>%
  mutate(player = case_when(
    winner_name %in% players ~ winner_name,
    loser_name %in% players ~ loser_name
  ),
  opponent_rank = case_when(
    winner_name == player ~ loser_rank,
    loser_name == player ~ winner_rank
  ),
  win = ifelse(winner_name == player, 1, 0)) %>%
  
  filter(!is.na(opponent_rank)) %>%
  
  mutate(rank_group = case_when(
    opponent_rank <= 10 ~ "Top 10",
    opponent_rank <= 20 ~ "11–20",
    opponent_rank <= 50 ~ "21–50",
    TRUE ~ "50+"
  )) %>%
  
  group_by(player, rank_group) %>%
  summarise(win_pct = mean(win), .groups = "drop")

ggplot(rank_tiers, aes(x = rank_group, y = win_pct, fill = player)) +
  geom_col(position = "dodge") +
  
  scale_fill_manual(values = c(
    "Carlos Alcaraz" = "#EEDC82",
    "Jannik Sinner" = "#4F7942"
  )) +
  
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent_format()) +
  
  labs(
    title = "Performance by Opponent Ranking Tier",
    subtitle = "How each player performs as competition level changes",
    x = "Opponent Ranking Tier",
    y = "Win Percentage"
  ) +
  
  theme_minimal()









Matches against top 50 opponents reveal how players perform at the highest level at a more consistent level. Here, Sinner seems to be the one with a stronger ability to compete against the current best.
Professional tennis is a unique sport in that it’s not played on a standard type of court surface. Yes, MLB stadiums have differing outfield fence dimensions and soccer pitches allow for a range of lengths, but nothing allows for the smallest of action to make the biggest of impact like the type of court surface a tennis match is being played on.
Let’s see, between Sinner and Alcaraz, who is the best across the three surface types.
Because court surface plays a major role in overall tennis performance


Code
library(lubridate)

rank_data <- all_matches %>%
  filter(winner_name %in% players | loser_name %in% players) %>%
  
  mutate(player = case_when(
    winner_name %in% players ~ winner_name,
    loser_name %in% players ~ loser_name
  ),
  
  player_rank = case_when(
    winner_name == player ~ winner_rank,
    loser_name == player ~ loser_rank
  ),
  
  year = year(ymd(tourney_date))) %>%
  
  filter(!is.na(player_rank)) %>%
  
  group_by(player, year) %>%
  summarise(avg_rank = mean(player_rank, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop")

ggplot(rank_data, aes(x = year, y = avg_rank, color = player)) +
  
  geom_step(size = 1.2) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  
  scale_color_manual(values = c(
    "Carlos Alcaraz" = "#EEDC82",
    "Jannik Sinner" = "#4F7942"
  )) +
  
  # Flip axis so rank 1 is at top
  scale_y_reverse() +
  
  labs(
    title = "ATP Ranking Progression (2020–2024)",
    subtitle = "Tracking the rise of Alcaraz and Sinner",
    x = "Year",
    y = "Average Ranking"
  ) +
  
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 14),
    legend.title = element_blank()
  )


Warning: Using `size` aesthetic for lines was deprecated in ggplot2 3.4.0.
ℹ Please use `linewidth` instead.











Code
bubble_data <- all_matches %>%
  filter(winner_name %in% players | loser_name %in% players) %>%
  
  mutate(player = case_when(
    winner_name %in% players ~ winner_name,
    loser_name %in% players ~ loser_name
  ),
  
  opponent_rank = case_when(
    winner_name == player ~ loser_rank,
    loser_name == player ~ winner_rank
  ),
  
  win = ifelse(winner_name == player, 1, 0)) %>%
  
  filter(!is.na(opponent_rank)) %>%
  
  mutate(rank_group = case_when(
    opponent_rank <= 10 ~ "Top 10",
    opponent_rank <= 20 ~ "11–20",
    opponent_rank <= 50 ~ "21–50",
    TRUE ~ "50+"
  )) %>%
  
  group_by(player, rank_group) %>%
  summarise(
    win_pct = mean(win),
    matches = n(),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

ggplot(bubble_data, aes(x = rank_group, y = win_pct, size = matches, color = player)) +
  
  geom_point(alpha = 0.7) +
  
  scale_size(range = c(4, 12)) +
  
  scale_color_manual(values = c(
    "Carlos Alcaraz" = "#EEDC82",
    "Jannik Sinner" = "#4F7942"
  )) +
  
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent_format()) +
  
  labs(
    title = "Performance vs Competition Level",
    subtitle = "Win percentage, opponent strength, and match volume",
    x = "Opponent Ranking Tier",
    y = "Win Percentage",
    size = "Matches Played"
  ) +
  
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 14),
    legend.title = element_blank()
  )
